content.text: The Traceability Problem

When an eval case fails in CI or a production incident is reported, the investigation always leads to the same question: which commit introduced this behavior and which eval cases are supposed to guard against it? Without explicit links between prompt commits and eval cases, the answer requires reading through git log output and source files by hand — a slow process during an incident.

Commit message trailers (Eval-case: and Refs:) create machine-queryable links that make traceability a script rather than a manual search. The discipline also enforces a practice: every prompt change must be accompanied by at least one eval case that exercises the changed behavior.

attrs.alt: Traceability chain from incident → eval case → eval commit → prompt commit with footer trailers

content.text: The Eval-case Footer Trailer

The Eval-case trailer in a commit message lists the IDs of eval cases that exercise the behavior introduced or changed by this commit. It is added to both the prompt commit (which changes the behavior) and the eval commit (which adds the test).

# Prompt commit — references existing eval cases that now pass with the fix
prompt(date-extraction): add disambiguation rule for ambiguous years

When a year is ambiguous (e.g. "23" could be 1923 or 2023), prefer
the closest future date. This fixes failures on queries like "schedule
for 3/15/23" where 2023 is the intended year.

Eval-case: eval-date-007, eval-date-008
Refs: #412
Safety-reviewed-by: @bob

# Eval commit — references the case being added
eval(date-extraction): add test for ambiguous year disambiguation

Covers the production failure from incident-2024-0115 where the model
interpreted "23" as 1923 in scheduling contexts.

Eval-case: eval-date-007
Incident: 2024-0115
Refs: #412

The same Refs: number (the issue or PR number) in both commits creates a three-way link: the issue/PR, the eval commit, and the prompt commit all reference each other. This makes the complete fix traceable from a single entry point.
What to Include in the Commit Body

The commit body for a prompt change should answer two questions: what was the problem with the previous behavior, and why does the new instruction address it? The 'what changed' is already visible in the diff — the body should focus on the 'why'.
Body element
Answers
Example
Previous behavior
What was happening before this commit?
"The model was returning dates in mixed formats (Jan 15, 15th, 2024-01-15)"
Root cause
Why was the old behavior wrong?
"The prompt did not specify an output format, so the model used the same format as the input"
Fix rationale
Why does the new instruction work?
"Explicitly requiring ISO 8601 in the output instruction eliminates format ambiguity without reducing accuracy"
Eval evidence
What do eval results show?
"Pass rate on date-format suite: 78% → 95% with this change"

# Well-written prompt commit body
prompt(date-extraction): require ISO 8601 for all date outputs

Previously, the model returned dates in whatever format was present in
the user's query. Queries like "schedule for next Tuesday" produced
"next Tuesday" as output rather than a parseable date, and "Jan 15"
queries produced "January 15, 2024" rather than "2024-01-15".

The root cause was that the prompt specified WHAT to extract but not
HOW to format the output. Downstream code was implementing format
normalization, creating fragile regex-based parsing.

Adding "Always return dates in ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DD)" to the
output section makes the constraint explicit without adding verbosity.

Eval results: date-format suite 78% → 95% pass rate.
No regression on the general accuracy suite (unchanged: 93%).

Eval-case: date-format-001, date-format-002, date-format-003
Safety-reviewed-by: @alice
Querying Traceability from the Command Line
With eval-case IDs in commit messages, standard git tools can answer traceability questions in seconds.

# Q: Which commit(s) claim to cover eval-date-007?
git log --all --oneline --grep="Eval-case:.*eval-date-007"

# Q: What eval cases does commit abc1234 cover?
git show abc1234 | grep "Eval-case:"

# Q: What prompt changes were made in the last two weeks?
git log --since="2 weeks ago" --oneline --grep="^prompt"

# Q: Which prompt changes reference issue #412?
git log --all --oneline --grep="Refs: #412" --grep="^prompt" --all-match

# Q: Which commits are linked to incident-2024-0115?
git log --all --oneline --grep="Incident: 2024-0115"

# Q: Find all prompt changes that have no Eval-case trailer (coverage gap)
git log --all --grep="^prompt" --format="%H %s" |
  while read sha subject; do
    if ! git show "$sha" | grep -q "Eval-case:"; then
      echo "NO EVAL CASE: $sha $subject"
    fi
  done

The last query in the example above is particularly useful for auditing: it finds prompt changes that were merged without linking any eval cases. These represent coverage gaps where a behavior change has no test coverage.
Adding Traceability to Existing Commits

When adopting this practice on a repo with an existing history, the past commits cannot be rewritten (it would rewrite history). Instead, maintain a traceability index file that maps eval case IDs to the commits that cover them.
Automated Coverage Gap Detection
A CI check that identifies prompt commits with no Eval-case trailer prevents coverage gaps from accumulating silently.
Best Practices Summary
Add Eval-case trailer to both sides:
the eval commit that adds the case AND the prompt commit that the case tests — this makes the link bidirectional
Add Incident trailer when applicable:
if the eval case was created in response to a production incident, link to the incident ID so the history is searchable
Keep eval case IDs stable:
never renumber eval cases — a renamed case breaks the commit message references in git history
Make the coverage check a CI gate:
not just a local reminder — a check that only runs locally will be skipped by developers who work quickly
Review the traceability index quarterly:
verify that prompt changes without trailers have been backdated into the index file
Traceability in Code Review

The traceability links in commit messages are most valuable when reviewers actively use them during code review. A pull request that changes a prompt should always have an associated eval commit — either in the same PR or referencing an already-merged eval commit.

<!-- PR review comment template for prompt changes -->

## Prompt Change Review Checklist

**Before approving a PR that contains prompt, guardrail, tool, or agent commits:**

- [ ] Each commit has an `Eval-case:` trailer listing the eval case IDs it covers
- [ ] I checked that each listed eval case ID exists in the `evals/` directory
- [ ] I ran `git log --grep="Eval-case:.*<case-id>"` to confirm these cases are not already covered by a conflicting commit
- [ ] The PR description explains what behavioral change this produces and why
- [ ] If this is a fix for a production issue, the `Incident:` trailer is present in both the eval and prompt commits
- [ ] The commit body explains the WHY, not just the WHAT (the diff already shows what changed)

**If any of these are missing:** request changes before approving.
Linking Prompt Versions to Deployed Configs

When a commit is deployed to production, the version deployed should be traceable back to the commit SHA and the eval case IDs that were validated. This closes the traceability chain from source commit all the way to live deployment.

With deployment metadata emitted on startup, an observability query can answer: "which eval cases were validated before the version currently running in production?" and "was this version deployed before or after eval-date-007 was added?"
Enforcing the Traceability Contract at PR Merge Time

The coverage gap check script shown earlier runs in CI on each PR. For teams that want stronger enforcement, the check can also run as a merge queue gate — blocking the merge if the final set of commits in the PR does not meet the traceability contract.

# .github/workflows/traceability-check.yml
name: Prompt Traceability Check

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main, 'release/**']
    paths:
      - 'src/prompts/**'
      - 'src/tools/**'
      - 'src/agent_configs/**'
      - 'src/guardrails/**'

jobs:
  check-eval-coverage:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0   # need full history for git log comparison

      - name: Check eval case coverage for GenAI commits
        run: python scripts/check_prompt_eval_coverage.py

      - name: Verify eval case IDs exist in evals/ directory
        run: |
          # Extract all Eval-case IDs from PR commits
          EVAL_IDS=$(git log origin/main..HEAD --format="%B" |
            grep "^Eval-case:" |
            sed 's/Eval-case: //' |
            tr ', ' '\n' |
            sort -u)

          MISSING=0
          while IFS= read -r eval_id; do
            [ -z "$eval_id" ] && continue
            if ! grep -rq ""id": "$eval_id"" evals/; then
              echo "MISSING: eval case '$eval_id' referenced in commit but not found in evals/"
              MISSING=$((MISSING + 1))
            fi
          done <<< "$EVAL_IDS"

          if [ "$MISSING" -gt 0 ]; then
            echo "$MISSING eval case ID(s) are referenced in commits but not defined in evals/"
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "All referenced eval case IDs exist in evals/ directory"

The second step — verifying that referenced eval case IDs actually exist in the evals/ directory — catches a common mistake: referencing an eval case ID that was planned but not yet written, or referencing a case that was deleted.
Traceability for Multi-Prompt Changes

Some features require changes to multiple prompts simultaneously: a system prompt, a user turn template, and a few-shot example. Each should be a separate commit with its own Eval-case trailer, rather than a single large commit covering all of them.

# Three commits for a multi-component feature:

# Commit 1: the system prompt change
prompt(summarization): add length constraint to output instructions
...
Eval-case: sum-length-001, sum-length-002

# Commit 2: the few-shot examples that reinforce the constraint
prompt(summarization/few-shot): add 3 examples demonstrating length constraint
...
Eval-case: sum-length-001

# Commit 3: the evaluation cases themselves (already merged first if possible)
eval(summarization): add length constraint test cases
...
Eval-case: sum-length-001, sum-length-002

# The result: running 'git log --grep="Eval-case:.*sum-length"' shows all three
# commits, making the complete change visible as a unit.

When the three commits are in the same PR, reviewers can see the complete behavioral change (prompts, examples, and tests) together. When they span multiple PRs, the shared eval case ID and Refs: number still links them.
Measuring Traceability Health
Traceability is a lagging metric: you only discover gaps during incidents. Track traceability health proactively with a weekly report.

# scripts/traceability_health_report.py
import subprocess, re
from collections import Counter

def audit_recent_genai_commits(since: str = "30 days ago") -> dict:
    output = subprocess.check_output([
        "git", "log", f"--since={since}", "--format=%H%n%B%n---END---",
        "--grep=^prompt\|^guardrail\|^tool\|^agent",
    ], text=True)

    blocks = output.split("---END---")
    total = 0
    with_eval_case = 0
    without_eval_case = []

    for block in blocks:
        lines = block.strip().splitlines()
        if not lines:
            continue
        sha = lines[0].strip()
        if not sha or len(sha) != 40:
            continue
        message = "\n".join(lines[1:])
        subject = lines[1] if len(lines) > 1 else ""
        # Only count GenAI type commits
        if not re.match(r'^(prompt|guardrail|tool|agent)', subject):
            continue
        total += 1
        if re.search(r'^Eval-case:', message, re.MULTILINE):
            with_eval_case += 1
        else:
            without_eval_case.append((sha[:8], subject[:60]))

    coverage_rate = with_eval_case / total if total > 0 else 1.0
    return {
        "period": since,
        "total_genai_commits": total,
        "with_eval_case": with_eval_case,
        "coverage_rate": coverage_rate,
        "missing_eval_case": without_eval_case,
    }

if __name__ == "__main__":
    report = audit_recent_genai_commits()
    print(f"Traceability health (last {report['period']}):")
    print(f"  GenAI commits: {report['total_genai_commits']}")
    print(f"  With Eval-case: {report['with_eval_case']} ({report['coverage_rate']:.0%})")
    if report['missing_eval_case']:
        print("  Missing Eval-case trailer:")
        for sha, subject in report['missing_eval_case']:
            print(f"    {sha}: {subject}")

A coverage rate below 80% indicates the practice has not been adopted consistently. Share this report in weekly retrospectives to create visibility and drive improvement without requiring a hard enforcement change that might block work.
Summary

Commit messages that link prompt changes to eval cases through Eval-case trailer syntax create a bidirectional traceability chain: given an eval failure you can find the originating commit, and given a commit you can find which eval cases it is responsible for. The practice enforces the discipline that every prompt behavioral change ships with eval coverage, and it makes post-incident investigation a command-line query rather than a manual grep through source history. The coverage gap detector in CI prevents the pattern from decaying over time.
Traceability rule:

every prompt, guardrail, tool, or agent commit that changes observable model behavior must include an Eval-case trailer. A commit that changes behavior without a linked eval case is untestable by definition.
